author: aapoalas

body: **issue:** This should use a primordial, I think. Same for `.set()` below.
author: aapoalas

body:
**suggestion:** One total overkill way to do this would be through a `Map<string, WeakRef<Uint32Array>` to weakly hold the `componentsBuf`s, and then a `FinalizationRegistry` with the `componentsBuf` registered in it and their held values being the URL strings.

Now, as long as a URL object holding this particular URL string serialized still exists, the `Map` will still keep its serialization reachable through the `WeakRef`. When the URL object gets GC'd, the `WeakMap` also gets the URL string key removed.

This would not improve performance (rather, would make it worse) for cases where the same URL is parsed occasionally but not held, and GC fires in between the creations. In that case the WeakMap would always be found empty.

Alternatively, the LRU cache could be made to keep order by doing `hrefComponentsMap.delete(url); hrefComponentsMap.set(url, components);` in the first if branch. Then the `hrefComponentsMap.keys().next().value` method would work properly for removing the "most ancient" cached `componentsBuf` from the cache.

body:
Looking good.

We currently the `SqliteBackedCache` is created unconditionally on startup. This involves creating an fs dir, and an sqlite db. Let's only create it on the first access of an op that requires the cache. This reduces startup cost, and defers issues for where the cache dir may not be writable to the actual call site of the cache API.
author: lucacasonato

author: lucacasonato

body:
This `put` is racy. If a user inserts an item into the cache with a pending body, then attempts to read that item from the cache, the item read will either error (no such file), or will return partial body data.

We need to change how puts work:
1. When calling `cache.put()` you create a resource on disk for that cache, with an arbitrary name.
2. Then create a body writer resource containing the file handle, request / resp metadata, and a timestamp indicating when the `cache.put` was started.
3. The body is then written to the file.
4. When the body stream is closed, you insert the metadata into sqlite, _if_ the timestamp in the resource is larger than the last inserted timestamp for that URL in sqlite.

This prevents the race, as other callers will not be able to see the entry until the entire body data is written. Additionally this prevents a scenario where two `cache.put` operations are started in close proximity, but the one that is later finishes before the earlier one, and then the data from the later operation is lost.

author: satyarohith

body:
No, not necessarily. Take the following case:

1. start `cache.put(resp1)`
2. start `cache.put(resp2)`
3. resp2 body is finished
4. `cache.put(resp2)` finishes and metadata is inserted into the db
5. resp1 body is finished
6. `cache.put(resp1)` finishes, but metadata should not be inserted into the db because this operation was started prior to `cache.put(resp2)`, which finished already

The timestamp is just a proxy for global insertion order. Instead of a timestamp you could also use a system global number that increments each time anyone calls `cache.put` - that just isn't very practical, so you can use a timestamp as a good proxy.

author: lucacasonato

body:
Currently the hash is just based off the request URL. This still results in a race:

1. `cache.put` with req + resp1 starts
2. `cache.put` with resp1 completes
3. `cache.match` with req. This returns resp1.
4. `cache.put` with req + resp2 starts
5. `cache.match` with req. This returns resp1, but with a corrupt body.

To fix this, we need to use a unique string as the key for the storage file for each request. Maybe just a v4 UUID.

author: billywhizz

body:
I'm not aware of any user visible difference between CoW and duplicates. 

FWIW the Rust standard library tries CoW too but it seems to fallback almost all the time because clonefile expects the destination file to not exist / be unlinked. https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/3fdd578d72a24d4efc2fe2ad18eec3b6ba72271e/library/std/src/sys/unix/fs.rs#L1609
author: littledivy

author: lucacasonato

body:
If `unlink` fails, `clonefile` might fail and fallback to io::copy. 
If `unlink` races with another `open` with create, `clonefile` will fallback to io::copy.
In fact, I don't think we need the `chmod` call. Rust doesn't do it https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/3fdd578d72a24d4efc2fe2ad18eec3b6ba72271e/library/std/src/sys/unix/fs.rs#L1609
author: littledivy

body: yes - that makes sense. APFS on mac seems to support COW out of the box. i just wonder if OS reports disk usage etc. differently when you use clonefile and maybe this causing confusion? whenever the new file is changed, a copy will be made by the OS anyway so effect should be the same.
author: billywhizz

body:
i don't think it matters for the unlink as clonefile will fail if unlink fails but if we fail to set mode correctly, then the whole operation should probably return an error or we should do some cleanup before falling back to normal copy (where it will likely fail again)?

tbh not sure i can think of a situation where we have managed to create the new file but aren't able to set the mode flags. :thinking:

We can not trust the `content-length` header. This code needs to be written in a way that `content-length` can be used as a size indication as a performance optimization, but the final size of the buffer is still determined by the size and number of actual chunks.

Specifically we need to deal with the cases:
- totalLength > `content-length`
- totalLength < `content-length`

body:
`Object.keys` is exactly what we need here since it works for sparse arrays. It's the reason of the performance improvement. See the following snippet

```js
const x = new Array(5);
Object.keys(x); // [] empty
const keys = x.keys(); // Array#keys

keys.next(); // value: 0
keys.next(); // value: 1
// ... And so on.
```
author: marcosc90

body: Why do we need special handling for Array type?
author: kt3k

body:
`Array` is the only one of those types that can have `empty items` so we need to handle it in a special way.

1. Using `for` loop is faster
2. There's no clean way of breaking out of the iterator from `.entryHandler`
3. There's no way to advance the iterator to a specific index, without iterating through the items, which is what we need to handle sparse arrays in a fast way

I had a working code for 2 & 3 but was way too hacky/ugly and slower than this implementation.

body:
I think we'll need to store what version each npm package references resolves to, then also do the same for each npm version requirement in each npm dependency.

When loading a lockfile, we can create an `NpmResolutionSnapshot` from that lockfile. It would be great if the lockfile can work in an additive way so that multiple scripts and even running npm package binaries in the repo can all add to the lockfile. Then if the user wants to "reset" it, they could delete the lockfile and re-run everything again.
author: dsherret

body:
> I think we'll need to store what version each npm package references resolves to, then also do the same for each npm version requirement in each npm dependency.

I'm not so knowledgable about this part. I'll be happy to discuss it offline.

> When loading a lockfile, we can create an NpmResolutionSnapshot from that lockfile. It would be great if the lockfile can work in an additive way so that multiple scripts and even running npm package binaries in the repo can all add to the lockfile. Then if the user wants to "reset" it, they could delete the lockfile and re-run everything again.

I believe that's the current behavior when you pass `--lock-write`. Also I think we could leverage information from lockfile to download all packages upfront instead of going through current discovery phase (which I think could be faster?)

There is no guarantee that the resource is at least 64 KB. The only constraint that matches all resources is min=0 max=infinite, which is `(0, None)`

body:
Max is an upper bound. We don't necessarily want to or can allocate that much space. The idea behind this logic is that we allocate the exact size we need if we know it, and otherwise we start at the lesser of `min` or `grow_length`. This prevents initial over-allocation.

As such I don't think this change is necessarily beneficial.
author: lucacasonato

body:
Sorry for not responding to this earlier. The logic in `op_read_all` is correct. The size_hint for the HTTP resources is just not. It currently is:

```
  fn size_hint(&self) -> (u64, Option<u64>) {
    (0, self.size)
  }
```

but it should be

```
  fn size_hint(&self) -> (u64, Option<u64>) {
    (self.size.unwrap_or(0), self.size)
  }
```

If the **exact** size is known (`self.size == Some(_)`), then the upper and lower bound should both be set to that exact size. Once you do this the existing `op_read_all` optimisation starts working.

This is already the case for the `size_hint()` in `ext/http`.
author: lucacasonato

body:
`buf.split_off()` is wildly inefficient for the `offset > 0` case (creates new vec, copies data, leaves old vec's capacity unchanged, i.e., wasted.)

Perhaps a better way to handle this is to maintain a vec of vecs, then join them at the end. That's `O(n)` but that's better than `O(n*m)`.

body:
Tangential and general observations:

1. Ideally omit the `?? new Uint8Array()` and have Rust land take e.g. an `Option<&[u8]>`. Or doesn't that work with fast calls?

2. It should be faster to `const { op_try_flash_respond_chuncked } = core.ops` at the top level and then call that,  otherwise V8 has to check if the `core` and `ops` objects changed on every call.

A caveat with (2) is that in results in more GC roots, meaning more work for the GC, but with concurrent marking it's probably still a win because the cost is paid by another thread.

/end tangent
author: bnoordhuis

body: Consider using `write!()` on a `&mut [u8]`, that saves a `String` heap allocation.
author: bnoordhuis

body:
It's probably better to try and combine small responses into a single slice. Three separate writes means:

1. Three system calls (expensive), and
2. Three TCP packets when Nagle is disabled (super expensive)

Copying bytes is fast so don't pick a too low limit. A 1024 or 2048 bytes cutoff point is probably a good choice.
author: bnoordhuis

body: 1. yeah that doesn't work with fast calls. Also `new Uint8Array` means the chunk ends with `0\r\n\r\n` without any special case for `Option`

author: littledivy

body:
I'm a bit queasy about the `__proto__` usage, given that Deno doesn't support it normally. eg. there's another PR open about how to handle NPM libraries that rely on `__proto__` and thus fail in odd ways when run in Deno through `npm:` imports.

Would it be an option to use `ObjectAssign(ObjectCreate(null), defaultValues)`?
author: aapoalas

body:
`__proto__` is something [defined in the ECMAScript spec](https://tc39.es/ecma262/#sec-runtime-semantics-propertydefinitionevaluation), and that file is written in JS, a language that conforms to the ECMAScript spec, so it doesn't really matter whether Deno supports it when running user code I think.

> Would it be an option to use `ObjectAssign(ObjectCreate(null), defaultValues)`?

It would be an option, but it typically has much worse performance on current versions of V8. IMHO it's also less readable.
author: aduh95

body:
There are two spec for `__proto__` in ECMAScript [since ES2022](https://github.com/tc39/ecma262/pull/2125)

1. Set the `[[Prototype]]` of the object by using it in object literals
2. Change the `[[Prototype]]` of the object by `Object.prototype.__proto__` accessor property (normative optional, legacy)

The first one works fine in Deno.

body:
Sure, will do that tomorrow. I thouht I did somewhere in detail, but I can't find it.

Essentially "copy" packages are packages that have different peer dependency resolution than another package with the same name and resolution. In other words, a package is only a "copy" if it's a duplicate of another package for the purpose of having different peer dependency resolution. In pnpm this is done by creating package folders like `foo@1.0.0_bar@1.0.0+baz@1.0.0` (https://pnpm.io/how-peers-are-resolved), but we just do `foo@1.0.0_1`, `foo@1.0.0_2`, `foo@1.0.0_3`, etc.. because it's much shorter (reduces chance of hitting windows file path limit) and the folder can be reused with a different resolution in the future if necessary.

body:
It used to be the case that passing SABs to Rust didn't work, because `ZeroCopyBuf` explicitly disallowed SABs. It seems like #15922 made this possible, and apparently no one noticed before now.

Accessing SAB data as a `&[u8]` or `&mut [u8]` is unsound because the buffer could be accessed or modified at any point from a worker. It has essentially the same safety guarantees as transmuting a `&[AtomicU8]`.

body:
It needs to dispatch:
```
match type(buffer) {
 SharedArrayBuffer => SharedArrayBufferPrototypeGetByteLength(buffer),
 ArrayBuffer => ArrayBufferPrototypeGetByteLength(buffer),
 TypedArray => TypeArrayPrototypeGetByteLength(buffer),
 DataView => DataViewPrototypeGetByteLength(buffer)
}
```

body:
This optimisation of picking out the data pointer directly from the `ArrayBuffer` instead of getting the `BackingStore` and taking the data pointer from there is a double-edged sword for externally backed zero-sized buffers:
1. This method will return a null pointer for those, even though the backing store pointer is something else. So, essentially this breaks getting the pointer out of externally backed zero-sized buffers.
2. Fast API calls likewise get a null pointer for externally backed zero-sized buffers. So, this essentially unifies how our FFI calls work with externally backed zero-sized buffers, removing a potentially very confusing bug.

body:
Tried (Node specific) Url object, and then it seemed throwing `ERR_INVALID_ARG_VALUE`

```js
import url from "url";
import { createRequire } from "module";
const u = url.parse(import.meta.url);
const require = createRequire(u);
```

This fails with:
```shellsession
$ node a.mjs
node:internal/errors:477
    ErrorCaptureStackTrace(err);
    ^

TypeError [ERR_INVALID_ARG_VALUE]: The argument 'filename' must be a file URL object, file URL string, or absolute path string. Received Url {
  protocol: 'file:',
  slashes: true,
  auth: null,
  host: '',
  port: null,
  hostname: '',
  hash: null,
  search: null...
    at new NodeError (node:internal/errors:387:5)
    at createRequire (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:1239:11)
    at file:///Users/kt3k/a.mjs:4:17
    at ModuleJob.run (node:internal/modules/esm/module_job:193:25)
    at async Promise.all (index 0)
    at async ESMLoader.import (node:internal/modules/esm/loader:541:24)
    at async loadESM (node:internal/process/esm_loader:91:5)
    at async handleMainPromise (node:internal/modules/run_main:65:12) {
  code: 'ERR_INVALID_ARG_VALUE'
}
```

So I'm removing the TODO comment instead.

body:
This is related to #16161. IIUC `ArrayBuffer::data` will return `None` for all empty buffers, in which case most APIs would expect `&[]` or `&mut []`.

For FFI APIs, however, this might break things, and it might need the `#[op]` macro to also support `Option<&[u8]>`.

body:
Not really. Only the ptr_of op is probably interested in the real pointer behind the slice instead of being interested in the slice itself.

There's just no good way to (currently at least) explain this to serde_v8 since while a plain pointer would be accurate to what the op needs, it doesn't really explain the JS param type.

body: `*const u8` does not suggest that the pointed value is _not_ mutable. You can safely cast it to `*mut u8`. It's just a hint that we are only dealing with the non-mut reference in the current context.
author: littledivy

body:
Yes, I'm just thinking that there might be a day when `*const u8` is needed to stand in for some V8 API type with Fast API support that offers a pointer without mutable access. Admittedly this is quite an unlikely thing scenario, but still a theoretical possibility.

I guess another way to go about this would use some transparent newtype like to tell the various pointer types apart in the ops macro:
```rs
#[repr(transparent)]
struct Uint8ArrayPointer(*const u8)
```

But yeah, since this is an unlikely scenario it doesn't really matter.

a is null, b is not
- `a === b` always false

a is not null, b is null
- `a === b` always false

a is null, b is null
- `a === b` always true

so the first `if` branch should also just cover the null cases right?

body: What kind of guarantee does Deno have that the fast api typed array will always be available? If not, it would panic.
author: anonrig

body:
If you refer to the alignment, this here is sort of safe but not: V8 itself guarantees that all TypedArrays are always 32-bit aligned and based on some limited testing the real guarantee is actually that all TypedArrays will be 64-bit aligned on a 64-bit machine (tested on Linux only though).

An issue is, however, that Deno FFI makes it possible to make `ArrayBuffer`s with any alignment. So, while it's usually okay to trust that the alignment is correct it can theoretically be unsafe to do so. The unwrapping should be removed.

If you refer to the FastApiTypedArray struct actually being there? V8 guarantees that it always calls Fast API calls with existent TypedArray pointers and never with null pointers.

body:
Not always - there is one case where hyper knows the size of the response body up front (through `content-length` header on the resp), where it will drop the body once that content length has been reached, regardless of if the stream is complete or not. This is expected behaviour, but it means that if you stream a body with an up front known size (eg a `Blob`), `shutdown` will never succeed because the body (and by extension the receiver) will have dropped by the time we try to shutdown.

I could try to wire it up to only swallow the error if this specific case has happened (might be a bit tricky to detect though).
author: lucacasonato

body:
That's not correct. You can see this in action using this playground: https://dash.deno.com/playground/slow-sheep-54
TL;DR: Content-Length too small results in an errored response stream.

```
🦕 curl https://slow-sheep-54.deno.dev/     # No Content-Length
Hello World%                                                                    
🦕 curl https://slow-sheep-54.deno.dev/11   # Content-Length accurate.
Hello World%                                                                    
🦕 curl https://slow-sheep-54.deno.dev/100  # Content-Length larger than response.
curl: (92) HTTP/2 stream 0 was not closed cleanly: PROTOCOL_ERROR (err 1)
🦕 curl https://slow-sheep-54.deno.dev/1    # Content-Length smaller than response.
curl: (92) HTTP/2 stream 0 was not closed cleanly: PROTOCOL_ERROR (err 1)
```

```
struct Permissions(Arc<Mutex<Inner>>)

author: ry

body: Not every usage is replaced with `PermissionsContainer` - usages in `ext/` crates are using `Arc>` which depend on traits from various crates. I tried the approach you suggest but it doesn't worked.
author: bartlomieju

body: > usages in ext/ crates are using Arc> which depend on traits from various crates what's an example?
author: ry

body:
Examples:
- `ext/net/ops.rs`
- `ext/net/ops_tls.rs`
- `ext/fetch/lib.rs`

These ops don't have access to `Permissions` struct (because `runtime/` crate depends on `ext/net` and `ext/fetch`) and thus to `PermissionsContainer`. Extension crates define traits for permission checks that have matching signatures that are later implemented in `runtime/` for `Permissions` struct (and thus `PermissionsContainer` since it derefs to `Permissions`). This is somewhat optimal solution because it doesn't introduce tight coupling between `ext/` crates and `runtime/` thanks to Rust's trait system.
author: bartlomieju

How is changing Permissions to be `struct Permissions(Arc<Mutex<Inner>>)` going to affect that?

```ts
const cmd = new Deno.Command(execPath, {
  args: [subcommand, "--config=" + configFile],
  cwd: ROOT_PATH,

`main_module()` won't work for all relevant subcommands. `eval_command()` creates its main module on the fly. Moving that "fake" main module name into `main_module()` would be trivial, but confusing. Adding `build_with_main()` wasn't my first choice. Suggestions are welcome

body:
For direct calls to `resolve_main_module()`, you're right, but `resolve_main_module()` is also called by `resolve_file_header_overrides()`, which is called by

- `create_graph_and_maybe_check()`
- `ProcState::prepare_module_load()`
- `ProcState::create_graph_loader()`

These calls are unconditional, leaving us with `Option<Result>` because `resolve_file_header_overrides()` is called even if there is no main module.  
We could add a pre-filter to `resolve_file_header_overrides()` to get rid of the `Option<Result>`, but that'd leave us with a duplicate `match`. Thoughts?

body: Little bike-shedding maybe it should be `Deno.osUptime()`?
author: bartlomieju

body:
`Deno.osUptime()` is more clear and falls in line with the naming convention of `Deno.osRelease()`. I definitely prefer it over `Deno.uptime()`.

I would also like to bikeshed another alternative being `Deno.systemUptime()` (which is in line with `Deno.systemMemoryInfo()`. I don't have strong feelings either way.
author: lino-levan

body:
After checking few other places, I decided to go with `osUptime`, as:

> the uptime is a kernel internal value, which ticks up every cycle, it starts counting when the kernel has initialized. That is, when the first cycle has ended. Even before anything is mounted, directly after the bootloader gives control to the kernel image.

which means that IMO it should be treated as `Operating System` info, where `System` info should be more "hardware-like" related,  as in mem/cpu/peripherals etc.

body:
For resolving bare specifiers I think we'll need to do the following:

1. Parse all dependencies and dev dependencies in package.json via `NpmPackageReq::from_dependency_entry(key, value)` to get a list of npm package reqs.
2. Add these package requirements into the npm resolver.
3. From the npm resolver, get the exact version for the `NpmPackageReq`.
4. Create a map of `key` -> to the exact version specifier (ex. `npm:{specifier}@{version}`)... I think we'll need to inject these exact specifiers into the npm resolver as well to get those resolving (maybe as a first pass). We need to ensure these don't end up in the lockfile though. I'm not entirely sure about this point.
5. Use that mapping here.

The main reason is because npm package dependencies can have all kinds of fun stuff in the value field that doesn't work with npm specifiers, but also it causes us to eagerly resolve all the dependencies in the package.json at the same time.
author: dsherret

body: I was just thinking about this more and maybe we should be stricter with what's allowed in the current project's package.json and only allow version constraints that are supported in npm specifiers. It would simplify our implementation and not complicate the lockfile even more. I think maybe it's not a big deal to be strict because it's a package.json that the user has control over so they could easily edit their package.json and update it to work with Deno in case they do something that's not supported.
author: dsherret

body:
From https://github.com/rust-lang/rustfmt/issues/4681

> Note that `use crate::lexer;` and `use crate::lexer::{self};` are not actually equivalent: The latter only imports the lexer module, while the former looks in all namespaces, so it can import up to 3 items at once, e.g. a function, a module and a macro of the same name.

---

Also https://github.com/rust-lang/rustfmt/issues/5398

author: dsherret

body:
Hm. That's a valid concern. I'll take a look at the execution environment and see if we can detect that.

Obviously, continuing to do what Rust does is one type of expectable behavior. It's unfortunate that different kernels do this differently. So, I'm torn between it's surprising because it's different from Rust or it's surprising because of environmental inconsistencies.

Obviously, we could use `canonicalize()` and always resolve the symlinks, but that feels like the worst version.

body:
Looks mostly good to me aside from the few minor comments, though I do not know the context matter here at all essentially.

I do wonder a bit about how usable this will be: Since a static import is needed to include the worker file in the module, the worker file must be written as you've done in the test with specific testing for if the code is being run in worker or not. The main worker file will know it's being run as the main file but any further imports are going to have to know that in some other way.

Still, at least this enables workers for standalone at all. And if/when resource reference imports become possible by spec that will enable including the worker files that way without actually importing them in the main.

Is the double-clone needed? First a clone is made here, then a clone of this clone is made on every invocation of the `CreateWebWorkerCb` closure.

body:
`(module loader as &EmbedderModuleLoader)` is a deref without needing to `use std::ops::Deref`. I can use that instead if it makes it clearer.

`module_loader.clone()` would clone the `Rc`, which doesn't work because the `CreateWebWorkerCb` callback must be `Send`.

Looking at this again, it seems like the `EmbeddedModuleLoader` is never mutated after creation, and the eszip is already put behind an `Arc`. So I guess I could also put the `maybe_import_map_resolver` behind an arc, so cloning an `EmbeddedModuleLoader` is cheap.

author: piscisaureus

body:
@aapoalas suggested something similar in https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/17663#issuecomment-1426832462. But I have since realized that unreachable dynamic imports added somewhere on the main thread's module map wouldn't really work for web workers when typechecking is on.

It seems like when a module is added as a root of the module graph, with this command line argument, it is by default typechecked with the `"deno.window"` lib, but if it includes a triple-slash directive, it will use the directive's lib. For web workers, that is not ideal, but it's workable. However, when a module is reachable from the main thread's module graph, it will use the `"deno.window"` lib _in addition to any triple-slash directives_, resulting in duplicate type definitions. While I haven't tested the static analysis in the new version of eszip yet, I suspect it will work like that as well. Meaning that this "hack" wouldn't really work for web workers.
author: andreubotella

body:
> However, when a module is reachable from the main thread's module graph, it will use the "deno.window" lib in addition to any triple-slash directives, resulting in duplicate type definitions. While I haven't tested the static analysis in the new version of eszip yet, I suspect it will work like that as well. Meaning that this "hack" wouldn't really work for web workers.

That seems like a detail (potentially a bug?) that can be worked out or fixed. Type check them both with and witout default lib maybe?
author: piscisaureus

body: Maybe a bare import statement could be done from the main module that runs in the worker? If deno spots it there it can assume it is run from a worker.
author: piscisaureus

body:
There is no "main module that runs in the worker" since the `Worker` constructor isn't statically analysable (it can be overridden both locally and globally). This PR is essentially about providing that main worker module.

Not an easy problem to solve. On one hand you'd expect dynamic imports to be type checked, on the other hand you want to avoid them because they're probably made dynamic for a reason, f.ex. being platform specific. For the hack that I proposed, adding modules to the module graph for having them be available for Worker scripts, it's beneficial that they're not type checked but also their inclusion into the module graph must not place any type libs (`"deno.window"`) on them either...

The real answer would be the [asset reference proposal](https://github.com/tc39/proposal-asset-references) though even then the question of how to do types is a bit tough.
author: aapoalas

body:
I'd suggest to treat these as orthogonal problems: the fact that it is not possible for a module to declare a dependency on a worker script is a different problem that this PR doesn't need to solve. 

It is also a real problem though esp. when a "complete" eszip must be built (e.g. when deploying to deploy).
author: piscisaureus

body: Maybe we could have deno_graph analyze for Worker constructors, remove this flag, then have a requirement for `deno compile` that everything needs to be statically analyzable for now?
author: dsherret

body:
While Worker constructs are not 100% statically analysable due to the overriding issue, I do have to admit that it's probably a good idea. Overriding the constructor is anyways a very minor case, even less so with a `compile` usage on top.

If we leave it at that, supporting statically analysable dynamic imports and Workers without providing this flag, then the end result will be that users with more complex `import() + compile` or `Worker + compile` cases will be forced to use my hacky idea of eg. including arrays of `() => import("./static-name.ts")` functions in their code to provide those modules into the module graph.

That is not really the worst situation imaginable. I could live with that. But, I also am a great fan of hacky solutions that skirt the edge between what is possible with the tools given and what is proper.
author: aapoalas

body:
> The real answer would be the [asset reference proposal](https://github.com/tc39/proposal-asset-references) though even then the question of how to do types is a bit tough.

At this point the more relevant proposals for this are [module expressions](https://github.com/tc39/proposal-module-expressions), [module declarations](https://github.com/tc39/proposal-module-declarations) and [import reflection](https://github.com/tc39/proposal-import-reflection). Since those are actually progressing, there is a real concern of future-proofness with starting to statically analyse `Worker` specifiers.

Is it out of the question to just... allow compiled modules to use the runtime module loader and file system cache on the executing machine, and print warnings when it ever needs to do so / error if incapable? It will always be a subpar experience when something works with `deno run` but not with `deno compile`. I don't know if it's a goal to try (again) to use a stripped down executable with compiled scripts but that seems like a maintenance burden anyway.

body:
Hey, and thank you for the PR! Apologies that it's taken me so long to get around to reviewing it.

Code wise this looks good to me, and the API can be iterated upon (eg. would it after all be better to have a single API with overloaded address and network interface parameters). However, one thing that must be handled before merging is some basic sort of testing. At the very least the APIs themselves should be exercised, even if the sending and receiving of multicast messages isn't explicitly verified to work (through eg. another active listener).
author: aapoalas

body: Not sure if it would be useful or just a hindrance, but these types could use string template literal types to define acceptable addresses.
author: aapoalas

body: Would probably be a bit more performant to use individual parameters for the address and interface: Iterating through objects is a bit of a performance pain.
author: aapoalas

body: Is the loopback status of a socket commonly changed while it's in use? Would it make sense to actually set this up as a parameter?
author: aapoalas

body: It seems like the `group_address` itself isn't actually needed, and instead the V4/V6 choice could just be a boolean parameter.
author: aapoalas

body:
I wasn't sure about this either, but as nearly every API for multicasting I've seen has this as a method call, I felt it was the safest option. 

When I read about multicast loopback, it does seem hard to imagine that you'd be flicking it on and off all the time... but I'm hesitant to make it impossible just because of my limited imagination.
author: sgwilym

body: I'd never heard of or used these before! [Is this the thing you mean](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/2/template-literal-types.html)? What would using this feature for IP addresses look like?
author: sgwilym

body:
Yeah. IPv4 address could be typed (in a rough way) like this:
```ts
type IPv4Address = `${number}.${number}.${number}.${number}`;
```
This doesn't check that the numbers are between 0 and 255 but it's better than nothing.

That being said, this may just be a nuisance in some cases, like f.ex. reading an address from a JSON configuration file, as TS will just type the address as `string`.
author: aapoalas

body:
Yeah, turning this into a parameter would make f.ex. Node compat probably impossible to achieve.

That being said... Maybe the loopback option could still be placed in some options object here or there. The most relevant place might be the Dgram creation itself, since this was for the entire socket right, and not just some membership?

We can then just leave this method here as an extra API that isn't really recommended in the general case.
author: aapoalas

body:
I think this can be simplified:

```toml
default = ["snapshot"]

# Snapshot the internal runtime code into a V8 snapshot, instead of
# bootstrapping isolates from scratch at runtime.
snapshot = ["deno_core/include_js_files_for_snapshotting"]
# A feature that changes how startup snapshot is generated, that allows
# extending it in embedder crates.
snapshot_from_snapshot = []

# "fake" feature that allows to generate docs on docs.rs
docsrs = []
```

body: No because when resolving with deno_graph we may call `ensure_top_level_install`, then wait for the full graph to be resolved, then finally call `resolve_pending()`. This is the only place they're done together so maybe in the future we could consider a helper method, ~~but a helper method does make it more likely to accidentally call both~~. Also, the way the structs are right now with the lsp would require a bit of a big refactor to make this a helper method. The code isn't great at the moment and more refactoring in the future would be nice.

**question:** Do we know this set vector to not contain duplicates? If so `sort_unstable` might offer somewhat better performance and avoids allocations. https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.slice.html#method.sort_unstable

> It is typically faster than stable sorting, except in a few special cases, e.g., when the slice consists of several concatenated sorted sequences.

That special case does sound like this might hit that, though :)

body: The variable exists to describe that it's being skipped because it's not diagnosable and the explicit match for all entries is so a compiler error occurs when a new `MediaType` is added/removed/changed here so this code can be re-evaluated. For example, say `MediaType::Css` is added... there won't be an error here and someone might forget to update it. It was fairly common in the past that using `_ =>` would cause bugs so I try to avoid it except when it's likely using it won't cause bugs in the future due to enum changes.

body:
With this assert_path function the exception thrown is + console.log(err.code) after:

```bash
# ./target/debug/deno run /tmp/deno_tests/file.ts
TypeError: [ERR_INVALID_ARG_TYPE] The "path" argument must be of type string. Received type Number(7)
    at Object.dirname (internal:deno_node/polyfills/path/posix.ts:173:16)
    at file:///tmp/deno_tests/file.ts:7:14
exception code:  undefined
```

This output is similar to how NodeJS logs:

```bash
# node /tmp/deno_tests/file.ts
TypeError [ERR_INVALID_ARG_TYPE]: The "path" argument must be of type string. Received type number (7)
    at new NodeError (node:internal/errors:371:5)
    at validateString (node:internal/validators:119:11)
    at Object.dirname (node:path:1276:5)
    at Object.<anonymous> (/tmp/deno_tests/file.ts:7:14)
    at Module._compile (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:1101:14)
    at Object.Module._extensions..js (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:1153:10)
    at Module.load (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:981:32)
    at Function.Module._load (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:822:12)
    at Function.executeUserEntryPoint [as runMain] (node:internal/modules/run_main:81:12)
    at node:internal/main/run_main_module:17:47 {
  code: 'ERR_INVALID_ARG_TYPE'
}
exception code:  ERR_INVALID_ARG_TYPE
```

Deno is not like Node and has a random " also.. The code is handled correctly though since this exception is thrown in Javascript userland.

```bash
# deno run /tmp/deno_tests/file.ts
TypeError [ERR_INVALID_ARG_TYPE]" argument must be of type string. Received type number (7)
    at assertPath (https://deno.land/std@0.176.0/node/path/_util.ts:19:11)
    at Object.dirname (https://deno.land/std@0.176.0/node/path/posix.ts:223:3)
    at file:///tmp/deno_tests/file.ts:7:14
exception code:  ERR_INVALID_ARG_TYPE

This is honestly not a big surprise. For the most part, for JS this would be just some index searches from a string and then a string slice (pointer plus a few offsets).

For Rust, this is a copy of the string, then some index searches, followed by a string slice, and then finally a new copy.

body: getting a substring this way returns 0 to N bytes of the path string (where n = the `ending` var in this example). This is an issue because if the string contains multi-byte characters than you'll get broken characters. If you have a file path with an emoji for example, that would break.

body:
sorry for the confusion @bartlomieju, The ~340 ms is from the benchmark which calls the function 10 million times. It wasn't 340 ms for 1 call of the op.

I made the op function like this just to experiment

```
#[op]
pub fn op_node_path_posix_dirname(path: Value) -> Result<(), AnyError> {
  Ok(())
}
```

results in the following when executing the [benchmark](https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/17993/files#diff-78e39aa9d2d55ee7df70a488feaf0d59ee27038dfcefc06fedf5a814d075eb03) I added in this PR:

```bash
# ./target/release/deno run cli/bench/node/path/op_node_path_posix_dirname.js
time 704 ms rate 14204545
time 717 ms rate 13947001
time 727 ms rate 13755158
time 728 ms rate 13736263

# deno run cli/bench/node/path/op_node_path_posix_dirname.js
time 130 ms rate 76923076
time 127 ms rate 78740157
time 106 ms rate 94339622
time 106 ms rate 94339622
```

The point I wanted to confirm was that I couldn't beat JS runtime. The deserialization serde is doing to convert the path into a Value might be adding some time too.

My next step was to perform some proper profiling of the function to see what is really happening.

body: Thanks, but I don’t think we should do this. If anything, we could extract out what’s done here to a separate struct that’s more descriptive (probably not worth the effort), but I think it should still use a semaphore.
author: dsherret

body:
I feel like using a semaphore is more applicable here because the data is not behind the mutex and using a Mutex here makes it seem like the data should go into it instead of being readonly sychronously (which I don’t believe Mutex supports quite like this). Internally IIRC, tokio’s Mutex is just `Semaphore::new(1)` (I’m just on my phone). The unwrap is not a big deal because that would only panic if the semaphore is closed.

So overall, I don’t think we should use a mutex since it doesn’t convey intent as well as a semaphore in my opinion.
author: dsherret

body: I don't know... on my first look at the new container `Semaphore` made it seem more complicated than it turned out to be and a mutex would have been more digestible. Semaphore=1 has capability in terms disregarding ownership and more which you have to look around for, my understanding is to practically prefer mutexes if you're not using those. I think it has less to do with being technically contained in the mutex since most languages don't have that. This is 6 lines of encapsulated logic either way, I just think it could be less of a stop-and-look.
author: nayeemrmn

body: A semaphore with 1 permit is a pattern I’ve come across a lot. For me, mutex is more confusing because it’s not holding data. Here’s the example with mutex using a semaphore internally: https://github.com/tokio-rs/tokio/blob/ff2f286c120680f03182082a3fb97d69cd71d27d/tokio/src/sync/mutex.rs#L362

For the coverage tests it's a bit tricky to use `args_vec` because then a space is added after `--coverage=`, breaking the command. I can fix it with 
```rs
   .args(vec![
      "test ",
      "--quiet ",

 ```

body:
We need to do `.args_vec` when providing `tempdir.to_str()` because `.args` will will break if `tempdir.to_str()` has a space in it, which might occur on certain systems. It should be like this:

```
   .args_vec(vec![
      "test".to_string(),
      "--quiet".to_string(),
      format!("--coverage={}", tempdir.to_str().unwrap()),
    ])
```

We can work on making that less verbose in the future.

body:
I'm wondering because of this:

> The traditional rollback journal works by writing a copy of the original unchanged database content into a separate rollback journal file and then writing changes directly into the database file. In the event of a crash or [ROLLBACK](https://www.sqlite.org/lang_transaction.html), the original content contained in the rollback journal is played back into the database file to revert the database file to its original state. The [COMMIT](https://www.sqlite.org/lang_transaction.html) occurs when the rollback journal is deleted.
> 
> The WAL approach inverts this. The original content is preserved in the database file and the changes are appended into a separate WAL file. A [COMMIT](https://www.sqlite.org/lang_transaction.html) occurs when a special record indicating a commit is appended to the WAL. Thus a COMMIT can happen without ever writing to the original database, which allows readers to continue operating from the original unaltered database while changes are simultaneously being committed into the WAL. Multiple transactions can be appended to the end of a single WAL file.

https://www.sqlite.org/wal.html
author: dsherret

body:
I believe this PR is correct. We may truncate some older cache WAL logs but I don't believe this is harmful long-term. I think there's a slightly higher chance of database corruption but I put together a PR to ensure that we don't silently fail and the user isn't confused:

https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/18330/files

In the future we may want to investigate one of the alternate journaling modes, but I think this is good for now.

LGTM.

For folks that will see this PR: this is a deliberate decision on our side to remove this API at this point. We asked you in the recent survey if you found this API useful and a vast majority of you said that it's not. Having WebGPU incurs 2ms startup time on every user even if you never use it. We will revisit relanding this API in the next few months, in the meantime we plan to open PRs to Rust compiler that would allows us to use weak linking to not incur the startup time performance on users who do not need WebGPU.

body:
This change broke the existing code, which is expected. Unfortunately, the `include_js_files!` documentation is out of sync with the actual implementation, which makes it more difficult to fix this problem.

Current docs:

```rs
/// /// Example with "dir" option (for "my_extension"):
/// ```ignore
/// include_js_files!(
///   dir "js",
///   "01_hello.js",
///   "02_goodbye.js",
/// )
/// // Produces following specifiers:
/// - "ext:my_extension/js/01_hello.js"
/// - "ext:my_extension/js/02_goodbye.js"
/// ```
```

Produces compiler errors like this one:

```
error: couldn't read ext/mine/js: Is a directory (os error 21)
  --> ext/mine/lib.rs:27:14
   |
27 |           .esm(include_js_files!(
   |  ______________^
28 | |             dir "js",
29 | |             "01_hello.js",
30 | |         ))
   | |_________^
   |
   = note: this error originates in the macro `include_str` which comes from the expansion of the macro `include_js_files` (in Nightly builds, run with -Z macro-backtrace for more info)

error: couldn't read ext/mine/01_hello.js: No such file or directory (os error 2)
  --> ext/mine/lib.rs:27:14
   |
27 |           .esm(include_js_files!(
   |  ______________^
28 | |             dir "js",
29 | |             "01_hello.js",
30 | |         ))
   | |_________^
   |
   = note: this error originates in the macro `include_str` which comes from the expansion of the macro `include_js_files` (in Nightly builds, run with -Z macro-backtrace for more info)
```
author: bajtos

body: Sorry for that @bajtos. Could you help me spot here what's the mistake in docs?
author: bartlomieju

body:
@bartlomieju sorry for posting a comment that's not actionable; I'll do better next time 🙈 

The new version of `include_js_files!` requires the extension name (`$name:ident`) as the first argument.

I think this is the correct example:

```rs
include_js_files!(
   "my-extension",
   dir "js",
   "01_hello.js",
   "02_goodbye.js",
)
```

In the end, I decided to bite the bullet and upgrade my code to use `deno_core::extension!` macro. It wasn't that much extra work, kudos for making the `extension!` macro easy to use outside of the Deno repo too!
author: bajtos

body: Ah I see, sorry again for the breaking change. We did all these changes to improve startup performance - both for Deno itself and all embedders. The `extension!` macro moves as much of the work to "build time" instead of "startup time" so hopefully you can see some reduction in your startup time too. Kudos go to @mmastrac for the macro :)
author: bartlomieju

body:
The changes make sense; I understand why you are making them 👍🏻 

Speaking about startup performance - is there any guide for embedders? I am particularly interested in learning how to set up our build to create a snapshot of all JS files we are bundling in our runtime to get that faster startup time you mentioned. I can probably figure this out by reading the source code of Deno core & cli, but it would be much nicer if there were a guide. Having said that, both the guide and snapshotting are pretty much "nice to have" for us right now.
author: bajtos

body: @bajtos there's no official guide - but long story short - you need to define `extension!` in your `build.rs` script when creating a snapshot and include all the files you want. Looking at `runtime/build.rs` and `cli/build.rs` (the latter creates a snapshot from existing snapshot) is the fastest way to see it in action. If you could open an issue about it we can look into adding some examples to `deno_core`.

author: littledivy

body: How would you use Box::into_raw here? There is no concrete type we know; this is an allocator impl for zlib internals so that it runs through the same global allocator in Rust.
author: littledivy
mmastrac

body:
Here's the thing: in `zfree` we don't know the size of the allocation. In your `unaligned_dealloc`, `Box::from_raw(ptr as *mut u8)` is `Box<u8>`. - you need a fat pointer though i.e. call `slice::from_raw_parts` with the size.

The reason I chose this implementation is because it's done in a popular Rust crate (flate2): https://github.com/rust-lang/flate2-rs/blob/main/src/ffi/c.rs#L68
author: littledivy

body:
If you set len to 0 for your aligned and unaligned code, it will crash trying to deallocate the null pointer. 

https://rust.godbolt.org/z/zjoY9hz5q
author: littledivy

body: I didn't realize that was from `flate2`, I'm good with that if the pattern is established. I'll resolve this and check my assumptions on the box approach for later -- you might be right here.
author: mmastrac

body: I think you're right here - and I think it's because the compiler neglected to warn me that I was casting from *mut [A] (the fat pointer) to *mut A (the thin pointer). So if I tried to use the pointer that came back from it I'd end up stomping over the fat pointer and beyond (as this is a pointer to the fat pointer's allocation, not the slice buffer). Good call!

body:
We decided to just use `.unwrap()`s instead of `.expect(...)` in the codebase because we haven't really encountered a scenario where it's better over `.unwrap()` since `.unwrap()` shows the line number and `.expect(...)` has more of a maintenance burden (and slowly increases the binary size).

```suggestion
    Self::initialize_connection(self.config, &conn, version).unwrap();
```

In scenarios where it's beneficial to have we need to provide any information, then use `.unwrap_or_else(|| panic!("Something {} ...", ...))`

If there are scenarios where it's useful to show a message to the user, then we probably shouldn't be using either `expect` or `unwrap`. In this case though, considering it's test code, I'd recommend unwrapping aggressively.

body:
In this case, the barrier enforces that the database is always initialized on the thread. If we don't wait for it, there's a small chance the main thread races and we end up with a DB that sometimes initializes on the main thread, and sometimes does on the background thread. 

To be honest, I'm not really sure that's a problem. While it ensures that things initialize consistently, the end result is that the cache starts initializing ASAP.

author: bartlomieju

body:
It seems like the `V8Slice` constraints don't make sense for `DetachedBuffer`: since ABs uniquely own their backing memory (unlike SABs) and the only way to obtain a `DetachedBuffer` is by detaching an AB, it should be perfectly safe to get slices to the backing store memory, as long as the Rust memory model is otherwise followed.

Edit: I guess one issue with this is that calling `to_v8` would need to invalidate the `DetachedBuffer` so that getting slices from it after that panics (or returns `None`/`Err`). This is a consequence of the fact that `ToV8` creates a JS-visible AB with the same backing store while not dropping the `DetachedBuffer`. But IMO this is fine, since it would most often happen when returning a `DetachedBuffer` from an op. And since `to_v8` takes `&mut self`, this can't pull the rug from under any existing slices, since there can't be any slices alive at the same time.
author: andreubotella

body: Can you explain why this dependency is needed?
author: bartlomieju

body:
> Can you explain why this dependency is needed?

The current text input used for `prompt`, based on `stdin.readSync`, has a lot of bugs that add up to poor UX. For example:
1. Pressing <kbd>←</kbd> inputs an ANSI escape rather than navigating to the previous character. Same goes for other arrow keys, <kbd>Home</kbd>,  <kbd>End</kbd>, etc.
2. All non-ASCII characters are somehow treated as being 2 characters when pressing backspace. The first backspace corrupts the character to U+FFFD replacement character, then the second backspace deletes that. Further, the cursor still moves back by 1 column after each backspace keypress, so what's visually displayed is inconsistent with the underlying value, and you can even backspace into the prompt text itself.
3. Backspacing moves the cursor back by 1 column even for double-physical-width characters (CJK, emojis, etc). This partially "fixes" the second issue for these characters by moving the cursor inside the character when it's corrupted to U+FFFD, which is arguably a better representation of what happened.
4. Zero-width characters such as ZWNJ take up 1 column when they should take up 0.

Possibly some of these should be opened as separate bug issues, ~~but presumably at least the first one is by-design~~ edit: actually, _is_ it by design? It makes sense for raw mode, but maybe non-raw-mode should treat those keypresses as instructions rather than raw input.

`rustyline` handles all those details (and probably a load more I haven't thought of) correctly. While it'd be possible to re-implement all of that in JS using stdin raw mode, it would add significant complexity and mean shipping a lot of extra JavaScript as part of the runtime. `rustyline` is already a dependency of the repl, so I don't think it should increase binary sizes (haven't tested that yet though)... it's possible it'd increase binary sizes of bundled executables, though, if those don't include the repl by default?

body: If we're supposed to read only a single key here, why do we need a buffer with size 1024? Could you also explain why you're setting the terminal to raw mode here?
author: bartlomieju

body:
> If we're supposed to read only a single key here, why do we need a buffer with size 1024? 

A single logical keypress (most notably, combos such as shift+insert/ctrl+v) can send an arbitrarily large amount of input to stdin. 1024 is just an arbitrary size that should typically be large enough to avoid problems but small enough to avoid undue memory usage.

For example, if we used a smaller buffer size like `10`:

```js
const { stdin } = Deno

const b = new Uint8Array(10)
stdin.setRaw(true)
stdin.readSync(b)
```

That can be broken (Deno exits with `Error: Io(Kind(InvalidData))`) by copying `字字字字` to the clipboard and pasting it with the next keypress, because the 3 UTF-8 bytes of the last `字` lie across the boundary.

> Could you also explain why you're setting the terminal to raw mode here?

Raw mode is necessary to capture ESC keypresses and to progress immediately after a key is pressed.

body:
This is the fix here. I'm not too happy about it because it adds a file system check, but it does solve the issue.

The reason I'm not happy about it is that the resolution code expects node_module paths everywhere like `/my-project/node_modules`. But in this [branch here](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/c162647020c26de1d27064b488ca11525f1bc4bf/ext/node/polyfills/01_require.js#L618-L626) we ignore that assumption and push a module directory to the list of paths instead (`/my-project/node_modules/my-module`). Then we reach this section here where we'd incorrectly append the package name to the path again resulting in `/my-project/node_modules/my-module/my-module` which doesn't exist and therefore panics.
author: marvinhagemeister

body:
I think we should add the following (all the methods on std::path::Path that interact with the file system https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/path/struct.Path.html):

```
{ path = "std::path::Path::canonicalize", reason = "File system operations should be done using NodeFs trait" },
{ path = "std::path::Path::is_dir", reason = "File system operations should be done using NodeFs trait" },
{ path = "std::path::Path::is_file", reason = "File system operations should be done using NodeFs trait" },
{ path = "std::path::Path::is_symlink", reason = "File system operations should be done using NodeFs trait" },
{ path = "std::path::Path::metadata", reason = "File system operations should be done using NodeFs trait" },
{ path = "std::path::Path::read_dir", reason = "File system operations should be done using NodeFs trait" },
{ path = "std::path::Path::read_link", reason = "File system operations should be done using NodeFs trait" },
{ path = "std::path::Path::symlink_metadata", reason = "File system operations should be done using NodeFs trait" },
{ path = "std::path::Path::try_exists", reason = "File system operations should be done using NodeFs trait" },
```

That way we'll get clippy errors when we go to accidentally use one of these in the future.

body:
I am very concerned about using a separate URL parser for user code and internal code. Because these parse differently, it is very likely we will run into security vulnerabilities down the line where the two different parsers parse the same string into different representations, which bypasses some security mechanism.

`rust-url` is very widely used throughout Deno (especially in module resolution, at startup), so improvements to `rust-url` would be much more impactful than replacing the implementation backing `URL`.

body:
Body in close frame is optional.

https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6455#section-5.5.1

Section 5.5.1:

> The Close frame MAY contain a body (the "Application data" portion of
   the frame) that indicates a reason for closing

ref: tungstenite https://github.com/snapview/tungstenite-rs/blob/92d65e11043f2a3bf18099ecc3cd7911e70bc456/src/protocol/frame/frame.rs#L347

Also, status code 1005 is not allowed for _normal_ close frames.

https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6455#section-7.4

Section 7.4:

> 1005 is a reserved value and MUST NOT be set as a status code in a
      Close control frame by an endpoint.  It is designated for use in
      applications expecting a status code to indicate that no status
      code was actually present.
author: littledivy

body:
This is the cleanest approach I could think of to translate the callback-style of new API to the older, async-style `serveHttp`. 

Each caller to `nextRequest` looks for a connection in the queue. If it finds one, it returns right away. If not, it  parks itself as a promise in `waiters`, and we wake one of them every time we get a new request, allowing one of them to grab the next request. If the waiter promise returns true, that means this `nextRequest` promise should grab a connection and resolve its promise. If it returns false, we're shutting down and it should return null.

It's possible we could simplify this and assert that there is never more than one `nextRequest` in-flight (I don't actually know if the existing API supports it - I need to write a test to confirm).

body: The new implementation of `upgradeWebSocket` does not seem correct. The actual upgrade should not begin until the token `Response` from the `Deno.upgradeWebSocket` function has been returned from the serve handler. `Deno.upgradeWebSocket` should not initiate I/O. body:
Ah yeah. This should probably be reflected in the documentation for `upgradeWebSocket`.

While I understand not wanting to break existing code, the old designs of these APIs are particularly nasty and deadlock-prone (`upgradeHttp` even has a deadlock warning in the doc page). `Deno.serve` is unstable and I think it's better to do things the right way in this API and migrate `serveHttp` over to the same in Deno 2.0. 

As `Deno.serve` is unstable, there should be no breaking API concerns. Creating a response that the user has to shuttle off to the right place is a massive footgun and it seems like a big mistake to do that here rather than update the documentation

body:
I think I have `OpState` available everywhere I'm using the slab, but that means chasing a lot of pointer just to get to what we need. This is at least part of why we're so slow serving HTTP. 

Is this just theoretical? Do we actually want to support multiple isolates on one thread, both serving HTTP? That seems like a recipe for extremely poor performance (and we're not testing this anywhere as far as I can see, so other things may just randomly break).

Is there a good reason we don't just say one isolate:one thread and be done with it? It unlocks a lot of performance (and we can do other fun things like putting isolate pointers in thread locals instead of having to pass them around). The OS give us a register dedicated to this magical space and we're missing out on performance if we don't use it.
author: mmastrac

body: Unlikely. I mean, if you are blocking on the calls required to generate the futures that you are returning, you are already doing something wrong and it'll fail in weird ways in other places. Resources should be able to create their associated read futures quickly and then defer to tokio to let them get polled to completion. If a resource is actually blocking in the synchronous part of the async call, then that resource is definitely busted and we need to fix it ASAP.
author: mmastrac

body: Yeah, totally. I'll keep this for a future task as we might see some wins here for some tasks.

body:
How is this correct? If a BYOB read occurs, the view shouldn't get detached for the duration of this read, right?

The view is still accessible to user code. Also, if the identity of the view is not preserved, why support BYOB in the first place?
author: lucacasonato

body:
`ReadableStreamBYOBReader.prototype.read` detaches the given buffer ([`ReadableByteStreamControllerPullInto`](https://streams.spec.whatwg.org/#readable-byte-stream-controller-pull-into) step 7) and returns a new one with the same backing store. And when you implement the underlying source, rather than responding with the number of bytes written into the given buffer, you can use `ReadableStreamBYOBRequest.prototype.respondWithNewView` to respond with a new buffer that points to the same backing store.

So the identity of the view or of the buffer are never preserved for BYOB readers, regardless of whether the underlying source detaches the buffer or writes in place into it. But the BYOB API instead lets you avoid reallocations of backing stores by always transferring the buffer in the boundary between reader and underlying source.
author: andreubotella

body:
Please add a brief doc comment.

Tangent: I copied the code into ChatGPT and asked it what it does, and it's pretty good. Maybe we don't need doc comments anymore soon :laughing:

> The NetworkBufferedStream is a custom wrapper around an asynchronous stream that implements AsyncRead and AsyncWrite. It is designed to provide additional buffering functionality to the wrapped stream. The primary use case for this struct is when you want to read a small amount of data from the beginning of a stream, process it, and then continue reading the rest of the stream.
> 
> The struct is generic over the type parameter S, which represents the underlying asynchronous stream that must implement the AsyncRead and Unpin traits.
>
> It has the following fields:
>
> - io: The underlying asynchronous stream.
> - initialized_len: The length of the valid data in the prefix buffer.
> - prefix_offset: The current position in the prefix buffer.
> - prefix: A fixed-size buffer used to store the initial data read from the stream.
> - prefix_read: A boolean flag to indicate if the prefix buffer has been fully read.
> 
> The struct provides the following methods:
> 
> - new: Constructs a new NetworkBufferedStream instance, taking the underlying stream, a prefix buffer, and the initialized_len as arguments.
> - current_slice: Returns a byte slice of the initialized data in the prefix buffer, from the current prefix_offset to the initialized_len.
> - into_inner: Consumes the NetworkBufferedStream and returns the underlying stream and the remaining data in the prefix buffer as a Bytes object.
> - poll_read: An implementation of the AsyncRead trait, which reads data from the prefix buffer first and then continues reading from the underlying stream once the prefix buffer is empty.
> - poll_write, poll_flush, poll_shutdown, is_write_vectored, poll_write_vectored: Implementations of the AsyncWrite trait, which delegate their functionality to the underlying stream.
>
> In summary, NetworkBufferedStream is a utility wrapper around an asynchronous stream that provides additional buffering functionality. It can be used when you need to read and process a small amount of data from the beginning of a stream before continuing to read the rest of the stream.
